rg_summary: In Forest Ridge Townhomes Corp. of Greensboro v. Heag Pain Mgmt. Ctr., P.A., 2026 N.C. App. LEXIS 253 (N.C. Ct. App. Mar. 18, 2026), counsel for the respondents cited irrelevant and inapplicable legal authority throughout an appellate brief, which the court in turn dismissed as meritless. Respondents’ counsel admitted that he had “supplemented” his traditional research “with research through Perplexity.AI,” for which the court chastised him for his “regrettable oversight” in failing to adequately review GenAI-generated work product. In so ruling, the court cited the North Carolina State Bar's formal opinion holding an attorney “individually responsible for reviewing, evaluating, and ultimately relying” on such work product. However, although petitioner moved to sanction respondents and their counsel for “substantially failing to comply” with the North Carolina Rules of Appellate Procedure and for bringing the appeal for an improper purpose, the court ultimately declined to impose sanctions, finding that the legal errors did not rise to the level of “gross . . . noncompliance.” The court affirmed the trial court's order on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
judge: Judge Tom Otis Murray | Judge Jefferson Griffin | Judge Christopher A. Freeman

rg_summary: In Doiban v. Or. Liquor & Cannabis Comm'n, 347 Or. Ct. App. 742 (Mar. 18, 2026), the petitioner’s counsel included in the opening brief “case citations to nonexistent cases and quotations that did not exist anywhere in Oregon case law.” In response to the order to show cause, the petitioner’s counsel apologized and explained that he failed to verify the citations and quotations from legal research delegated to his staff. He further explained that his office does not permit GenAI use for drafting legal documents or conducting legal research and that they did not use GenAI for actual drafting, although they did use it for administrative tasks. Counsel explained that he believes the errors were from what they thought were legitimate sources found through internet searches . Counsel and the law firm took steps to remedy the errors, including reviewing all citations in all court filings, restriction of citation to only Westlaw and Lexis databases or “other published and verified legal precedent,” and implementation of a “system of increased review” to permit additional time for verification and accuracy. Despite these proposed corrective actions, the court found that a $10,000 sanction on the petitioner’s counsel was warranted, particularly because the respondent’s counsel pointed out the errors, but the petitioner’s counsel did not acknowledge or address them until the court acted, thereby “minimiz[ing] the gravity of the situation.” The court allowed the petitioner to file a replacement brief, limited to the same arguments, relying only on the nonfabricated authorities.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Doiban v. Or. Liquor & Cannabis Comm’n, 2026 Ore. App. LEXIS 361 (Or. Ct. App. Mar. 18, 2026), petitioner’s counsel filed an opening brief in an administrative appeal that contained at least fifteen fabricated case citations and at least nine nonexistent quotations. Counsel explained that law firm staff used an “unverified search engine” for legal research after finding limited results on traditional platforms, but admitted that the resulting citations were not verified in Westlaw or Lexis before filing. Although counsel maintained that his office policy prohibits the use of GenAI for drafting or legal research, the court was skeptical that a “bad search engine” alone could account for the errors. Notably, opposing counsel flagged the fabricated citations months before oral argument, but petitioner's counsel did not respond or seek to correct them for seven months, acknowledging the errors only when questioned by the court at oral argument. The court found that sanctions were warranted under ORCP 17 C(3), which provides that an attorney who signs a brief certifies that its legal positions are warranted by existing law. The court imposed a $10,000 monetary sanction on counsel, permitted the filing of a replacement brief which cites only to the “five remaining [non-hallucinated] cases listed in the table of authorities” (provided that those cases are accurately described), and required counsel to certify that GenAI was not used to produce the redacted brief and that all citations had been personally verified.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms
judge: Presiding Judge Scott A. Shorr | Judge Steven R. Powers | Judge Ryan Timothy O’Conner

App. 742 (Mar. 18, 2026), the petitioner’s counsel included in the opening brief “case citations to nonexistent cases and quotations that did not exist anywhere in Oregon case law.” In response to the order to show cause, the petitioner’s counsel apologized and explained that he failed to verify the citations and quotations from legal research delegated to his staff. He further explained that his office does not permit GenAI use for drafting legal documents or conducting legal research and that they did not use GenAI for actual drafting, although they did use it for administrative tasks. Counsel explained that he believes the errors were from what they thought were legitimate sources found through internet searches . Counsel and the law firm took steps to remedy the errors, including reviewing all citations in all court filings, restriction of citation to only Westlaw and Lexis databases or “other published and verified legal precedent,” and implementation of a “system of increased review” to permit additional time for verification and accuracy. Despite these proposed corrective actions, the court found that a $10,000 sanction on the petitioner’s counsel was warranted, particularly because the respondent’s counsel pointed out the errors, but the petitioner’s counsel did not acknowledge or address them until the court acted, thereby “minimiz[ing] the gravity of the situation.” The court allowed the petitioner to file a replacement brief, limited to the same arguments, relying only on the nonfabricated authorities.

rg_summary: In Singletary v. SWBC Mortg. Corp., No. 25-20441, 2026 U.S. App. LEXIS 7895 (5th Cir. Mar. 17, 2026), the pro se appellant “relie[d] heavily on AI-hallucinated case law” in his filing. The court briefly addressed the misuse, warning that “[f]urther failure to check the accuracy of cited authority before filing with the Court may result in sanctions.” The court affirmed the district court’s judgment on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
judge: Judge W. Eugene Davis | Judge Cory T. Wilson | Judge Dana Douglas

rg_summary: In Hsien Chueh Yao v. Univ. of S. Fla. Bd. of Trs., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 55531 (M.D. Fla. Mar. 17, 2026), the pro se plaintiff submitted a renewed reply brief which again included a fabricated citation, despite prior warnings of potential sanctions and despite a certification in the brief that asserted “[n]o fictitious or unverified authorities are included [in the filing].” The court agreed that the citation could not be verified, but denied the defendant’s motion for sanctions because the defendant failed to comply with the mandatory safe harbor provision of Fed. R. Civ. P. 11(c)(2), which requires that a sanctions motion first be served on the opposing party and that the challenged filing be given twenty-one days to be withdrawn or corrected before the motion is filed with the court. On unrelated grounds, the court granted the defendants’ motions to dismiss.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Annabi v. Ocasio-Cortez, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 55061 (S.D.N.Y. Mar. 17, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed objections to a magistrate judge’s report and recommendation. The defendant pointed out that the plaintiff’s objections contained “numerous quotation and sourcing errors and other indicators of potential use of [GenAI] in preparing the filing.” The plaintiff acknowledged that he used GenAI in preparing his objections and apologized for belatedly filing his GenAI certifications (as required by Section 3(c) of the court's Individual Rules and Practices for Pro Se Litigants). The court appreciated the plaintiff’s acknowledgement and apology and declined to impose sanctions. On unrelated grounds, the court adopted the magistrate judge’s report and recommendation and granted the defendant's motion to dismiss.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Alonso v. Jackson, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 55384 (W.D. Wash. Mar. 17, 2026), the attorney for plaintiff filed a brief in support of a request of leave to amend which cited a “potentially fake case.” Defense counsel suspected the citation was “confabulated” by GenAI because two real cases with similar names existed in a different district. The court agreed that the cited case “cannot be located online” and that circumstances “suggest, but do not prove, the possibility of improper oversight of [GenAI] or another tool generating text from a large language model.” The court emphasized that “[c]iting nonexistent case law or misrepresenting the holdings of a case is making a false statement to a court” and that such conduct is sanctionable under Rule 11. The court warned plaintiffs’ counsel that it would issue an order to show cause why sanctions should not issue if they again submit fictitious legal authority. On unrelated grounds, the court granted the defendant’s motion to dismiss.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Pridgen v. Amazon.com Servs. LLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 54341 (D.N.J. Mar. 16, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed an opposition to the defendant's motion to dismiss; that opposition cited a nonexistent case to support the proposition that courts “routinely permit pro se plaintiffs to plead class claims.” Judge Padin stated that the court “does not tolerate hallucinated citations” and that “[l]itigants who use [GenAI] must disclose such use to this Court and litigants must review the filings for accuracy,” citing her Judicial Preferences standing order. The court further noted that the plaintiff’s citation to nonexistent authority “bolsters the Court’s conclusion” that the plaintiff could not adequately represent the interests of other litigants in the putative class action. The court warned that future filings containing hallucinated citations will be stricken and that the plaintiff may be subject to additional sanctions. The court granted the defendant's motion to dismiss on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Esterra Commons Venture, LLC v. Norton, 2026 Wash. App. LEXIS 543 (Wash. Ct. App. Mar. 16, 2026), the pro se appellant “quot[ed] fictitious statutory language, cite[d] inapplicable statutes, and relie[d] on cases that do not exist” throughout his appellate briefing. The court acknowledged the possibility that “such fake and non-existent legal authority was generated by [GenAI]” and warned that all litigants, including pro se parties, “are responsible to confirm their pleadings, motions, briefs, and other filings are accurate and do not contain any citations that are invented or hallucinated by [GenAI] or any other source.” The court did not impose sanctions and affirmed the trial court’s ruling on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
judge: Judge Linda W.Y. Coburn | Judge Lori K. Smith | Judge Bill Bowman

rg_summary: In Whiting v. City of Athens, Nos. 24-5918/5919, 25-5424, 2026 U.S. App. LEXIS 7479 (6th Cir. Mar. 13, 2026), the court observed that plaintiff-appellant’s attorneys’ briefs contained “over two dozen fake citations and misrepresentations of fact.” The court ordered the attorneys to show cause why they should not be sanctioned. Instead of responding to the show cause order, the attorneys argued that “the show cause order was ‘void on its face for failing to include a signature of an Article III judge,’ was ‘motivated by harassment of the Respondent attorneys,’ and ‘reflect[ed] illegal ex-parte [sic] communications within this Court.’” The court decided to sanction the attorneys under both its inherent authority and Fed. R. App. P. 38, which permits courts to award damages and single or double costs to the appellee if an appeal is frivolous. The court reasoned that misconduct such as misrepresenting caselaw or the record can render an appeal frivolous as argued and determined that the plaintiff-appellant acted in bad faith for relying on fake cases, acting with improper purpose by attempting to reach a result that is not possible under existing law, and arguing the show cause order instead of providing an explanation for the false citations. In determining the severity of the sanctions, the court considered four aggravating factors: (i) the attorneys engaged in further misconduct on appeal of sanctions orders; (ii) the attorneys have already been disciplined for lack of candor to the tribunal; (iii) the attorneys “defied [the] court’s order to show cause and refused to provide the information [the court] requested”; and (iv) the responses the attorneys filed “show[ed] a stunning lack of respect for this court, the members of the panel and their staffs, and the rule of law.” The court imposed the following sanctions: 1) the attorneys must reimburse the defendants-appellees for attorneys’ fees on appeal; 2) the attorneys must pay double costs to appellees; 3) appellees will file an accounting of costs and the attorneys can file responses or objections; and 4) the attorneys must individually pay the court $15,000.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Taylor v. Las Vegas Metro. Police Dep’t, No. 2:25-cv-00840-APG-NJK, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 51899 (D. Nev. Mar. 13, 2026), the pro se plaintiff included “hallucinogenic citations to non-existent cases” in his brief in opposition to the defendant’s motion to dismiss. The court warned that if the errors resulted from GenAI use, the failure to verify the accuracy of his case citations could expose the plaintiff to sanctions under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. Judge Gordon denied the defendants’ motions to dismiss and granted the plaintiff leave to file a second amended complaint on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Hartmann v. Davidson, No. 3:24-cv-02317-BT, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 51008 (N.D. Tex. Mar. 12, 2026), the court found that “[n]one of [the pro se plaintiff’s] cited authorities support his argument” for his Fifth Amendment claim in his brief in opposition to the defendants’ motion to dismiss. Magistrate Judge Rutherford noted that the errors were “typical of unverified, AI-generated filings” and that while GenAI use is not prohibited by any nationwide or local rule and that the plaintiff in fact disclosed his GenAI use, all parties are still required to conduct a “careful review of AI-generated statements and citations to ensure accuracy.” The court granted the defendant’s motion to dismiss on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In In re Obeginski, No. 09-26-00057-CV, 2026 Tex. App. LEXIS 2321 (Tex. App. Mar. 12, 2026), the relator filed a writ of mandamus requesting relief from the trial court’s imposition of sanctions against him for citing fictitious authority. The trial court had ordered the relator “when making ‘any filing of any plea, pleading, motion, brief, or similar’ in any capacity to ‘include with each filing . . . a copy of any and all legal authorities cited in the filing highlighting the portion of the legal authority attached which supports the proposition for which he cites the legal authority.’” The Court of Appeals of Texas found that “[o]rdering the person who cited fictitious cases to attach highlighted copies of the cited cases to his filings relates directly to the abuse found,” so the trial court did not abuse its discretion. The court denied the petition for writ of mandamus.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties
judge: Judge W. Scott Golemon | Judge Jay Wright | Judge Kent Chambers

rg_summary: In Suiter v. GM, No. 5:24-cv-00054, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 51280 (W.D. Va. Mar. 12, 2026), the pro se plaintiff’s briefs contained “numerous quotes that do not exist in the cited cases” and “characteristics that the court has observed in filings made with generative artificial intelligence programs.” Judge Yoon warned the plaintiff that future violations could result in sanctions. The court adopted the Magistrate Judge Hoppe’s R&R and granted the defendants’ motions for summary judgment in part and denied them in part on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In In re Nwaubani, No. 25-9517, 2026 WL 687194 (4th Cir. Mar. 11, 2026), the attorney representing the plaintiff on appeal cited a case in his opening brief that “did not exist.” In response to the court’s order to show cause, the attorney maintained that he intended to cite a different case and that the error was not the result of GenAI. His revised opening brief, however, simply omitted the argument that contained the nonexistent citation. Furthermore, the attorney’s reply brief contained two citations to nonexistent cases. The Standing Panel on Attorney Discipline issued a supplemental notice to show cause, where the attorney again claimed that the citations were errors and that he meant to cite different cases. Those cases, however, “appear[ed] to have minimal relevance to the facts or law” of the brief. After formal briefing and oral argument, the Standing Panel decided that the proper standard of proof for ethical violations is clear and convincing evidence, and that the District of Columbia Bar Rule of Professional Conduct 8.4(d) that prohibits “conduct that seriously interferes with the administration of justice” is the proper ethical rule to consider. The court found that regardless of whether the attorney used GenAI, “submitting a brief with nonexistent cases” where the “legal relevance of [the] existing cases to his arguments [] is tenuous at best” constitutes such conduct. The panel ultimately determined there was “clear and convincing evidence” that the attorney’s conduct violated the professional rules and the court’s Local Rule 46(g)(1)(c). As discipline, the court issued a public admonishment.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms
judge: Judge A. Marvin Quattlebaum Jr. | Judge Allison Jones Rushing | Judge DeAndrea G. Benjamin

rg_summary: In Traver v. GM Fin. Co., No. 25-CV-11890-AK, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 50026 (D. Mass. Mar. 11, 2026), the pro se plaintiffs cited nonexistent cases in their opposition to the defendants’ motion to dismiss. The court reminded the plaintiffs that they cannot make “false or fictitious representations to the Court” and ordered that in future filings they must certify that the citations are “not hallucinated or falsely represented.” Judge Kelley also warned that future submissions with nonexistent citations may result in sanctions. The court ultimately granted and denied in part the defendants’ motion to dismiss on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Roussell v. Bank of N.Y. Mellon, No. 4D2025-1309, 2026 Fla. App. LEXIS 1910 (Fla. 4th Dist. Ct. App. Mar. 11, 2026), the pro se appellant’s brief cited “thirteen different cases that do not exist” and “nine different cases that do exist, but do not stand for the proposition described by the appellant.” The court “expressly disregard[ed]” the nonexistent cases and reminded the litigants that while they may use GenAI to help prepare filings, they must verify the accuracy of their submissions, and the failure to do so may result in sanctions. The court affirmed on the merits without discussion.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
judge: Judge Spencer Levine | Judge Burton Conner | Judge Shannon K. Shaw

rg_summary: In McClellon v. E. Rickard, Warden of FCI Otisville, No. 24-CV-10053, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 50381 (S.D.N.Y. Mar. 11, 2026), a habeas petitioner objected to the report and recommendation (the “R&R”) issued by Magistrate Judge Barbara C. Moses, alleging that “the directive to file the R&R in ‘every court’ is unduly prejudicial and beyond the proper scope of relief.” However, Judge Broderick acknowledged that at least three “hallucinated” cases to which the petitioner cited were nonexistent and may have been included due to GenAI tools. The court disagreed that requiring copies of the R&R and order submitted in every other court would be “unduly prejudicial” and instead warned the petitioner that the failure to disclose GenAI use and the inclusion of hallucinated cases in future filings could result in sanctions. Judge Broderick adopted the remainder of the R&R and denied the petition and motions.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Hawkins v. DeSoto Indep. Sch. Dist., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 70417 (N.D. Tex. Mar. 11, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed a response to the defendants’ motions to dismiss in an employment discrimination and retaliation action that contained improperly cited cases, at least two of which did not exist. The court observed that these errors “are typical of unverified, AI-generated filings, which have become an increasing concern for courts in recent years.”  Magistrate Judge Rutherford reminded the plaintiff that while no federal or local rule explicitly prohibits the use of GenAI in litigation, all parties, including those proceeding pro se, “must fulfill their obligations under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11(b),” which “necessitates a careful review of AI-generated statements and citations to ensure accuracy.” The court further noted that Local Rule 7.2(f), adopted after the relevant pleadings were filed, requires parties to disclose the use of GenAI. The court recommended granting the defendants’ motions to dismiss on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Dillon v. Stephenson, No. CIV-25-484-SLP, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 50055 (W.D. Okla. Mar. 11, 2026), the court flagged that the plaintiffs included “incorrect citations” in their response to the defendant’s motion to dismiss. When the court asked plaintiffs’ counsel whether GenAI was used in connection with the response brief, they denied using such tools to “generate any content” but suggested using GenAI as a “research assistant.” However, the court found additional incorrect citations that “may have been the result of reliance on a generative-AI program.” Judge Palk admonished plaintiffs’ counsel and warned that future GenAI misconduct “will result in the imposition of sanctions and any other action the Court deems necessary.” The court granted the defendants’ motions to dismiss on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Hunter v. TForce Freight Inc., No. CV-26-00122-PHX-KML, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 48744 (D. Ariz. Mar. 10, 2026), the court noted that “some of [the plaintiff’s] filings [in connection with the defendant’s motion to dismiss] suggest he may be using [GenAI].” Judge Lanham reminded the parties that the court’s policy on GenAI use requires a disclosure and certification that the party has independently verified the filing’s accuracy and that any failure to follow the policy may result in sanctions. The court ultimately reminded the plaintiff that he “must comply with the court’s procedures regarding [GenAI]” when preparing his amended complaint and dismissed the plaintiff’s amended complaint (with leave to amend) on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In A.K. v. M.R., No. 25A-PO-2249, 2026 Ind. App. Unpub. LEXIS 360 (Ind. Ct. App. Mar. 10, 2026), the court suspected that the pro se appellant used GenAI to conduct research for his brief because the citations to statutes and case law included therein “[did] not support the propositions that [appellant] claims they do” and “[did] not correspond to the case names stated in the brief.” Judge Bailey declined to impose sanctions as the appellee did not request any, but cautioned against relying on GenAI tools without independent verification. The trial court’s decision was affirmed on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Allen v. Casper, No. 25 C 10438, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 48974 (N.D. Ill. Mar. 10, 2026), the pro se plaintiff submitted an opposition to the defendant’s motion to dismiss with “two fake cases and repeatedly inaccurate and false statements wholly bereft of support in the record—no doubt because of overreliance on AI.” The court found that these actions violated Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 and imposed $1,500 in sanctions. Judge Kendall granted the defendant’s motion to dismiss with prejudice on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Platt v. Volunteers of Am. Ohio, No. 1:25-cv-01866-SEB-KMB, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 48777 (S.D. Ind. Mar. 10, 2026), the court suspected that the pro se plaintiff used GenAI to prepare his response brief because it included citations that “either do not exist or are materially misrepresented.” The court considered sua sponte ordering the plaintiff to show cause for why he should not be sanctioned but decided not to because of his pro se status. Magistrate Judge Barr instead struck the plaintiff’s response brief from the record, granted the plaintiff leave to refile, and admonished the plaintiff that in the future he must comply with the federal and local rules or may face sanctions.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Cartagena v. Dixon, No. 1:25-cv-03552, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 49468 (S.D.N.Y. Mar. 10, 2026), the attorney-defendant included “pervasive citations to non-existent authority” in his briefing in support of the motion to dismiss. The defendant claimed that the errors were the result of using Protégé in LexisNexis, but LexisNexis confirmed that the defendant did not subscribe to Protégé. Judge Rochon noted that the defendant had received warnings and sanctions for similar litigation misconduct in other jurisdictions, and that those warnings, sanctions, and CLE courses “did not have an appreciable impact on [the defendant].” The court admonished the defendant with the “lengthy historic recitation” of his misconduct and warned that further misconduct could lead to sanctions or “a referral for discipline.” Judge Rochon granted and denied in part the defendant’s motion to dismiss on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Slay v. Ross, No. A25A2100, 2026 Ga. App. LEXIS 151 (Ga. Ct. App. Mar. 9, 2026), the court was “troubled by certain irregularities in the brief” filed by the appellant’s attorney, as the opening brief contained several hallucinated citations, leading the court to suspect GenAI misuse. The court noted that this was the attorney’s first infraction in this court (though the attorney had submitted a filing with “seemingly similar defects in federal district court”), and the errors did not “impede[] [the appellee’s] ability to respond.” Judge Markle therefore warned the attorney that submitting future filings with hallucinated citations may result in sanctions. The court affirmed the judgment on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
judge: Judge David Todd Markle | Judge Sara L. Doyle | Judge Wade Padgett

rg_summary: In Harwell v. WestCare Nev., Inc., No. 2:25-cv-00689-APG-BNW, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 47690 (D. Nev. Mar. 6, 2026), the court suspected that the pro se plaintiff prepared her complaint using GenAI because she cited a nonexistent case to support her negligence claim. Judge Gordon reminded the parties that “citing fake cases drafted by [GenAI] may violate [Fed. R. Civ. P. 11]” and “may result in sanctions” in the future. The court granted the defendant’s motion to dismiss and for a more definite statement in part on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In In re A.I. Task Force of the D.C. Courts, 2026 D.C. App. LEXIS 82 (Mar. 6, 2026), the District of Columbia Court of Appeals established a standing AI Task Force comprising 18 appointed members (including judges, court administrators, and IT staff) tasked with examining and developing best practices for “the use of AI in the courts,” expanding the courts’ use of AI, ensuring that AI “is used appropriately and the integrity of the court process is preserved and maintained,” and to “[d]evelop court rules with respect to disclosure, transparency, ethics, accuracy, authenticity[,] and certification of AI use in court pleadings and proceedings.”
rg_tags: Any AI Usage, Generative AI Usage
judge: Chief Judge Anna Elizabeth Blackburne-Rigsby | Judge Milton C. Lee

rg_summary: In Kattom v. Bondi, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 46975 (W.D. La. Mar. 6, 2026), the court sanctioned an attorney $1,000 for filing a motion for a temporary restraining order and accompanying memorandum containing hallucinated citations and incorrect quotations. The attorney used ChatGPT to draft the memorandum in support of the motion, which was filed on an expedited basis due to concerns that removal of the petitioner was imminent. Despite understanding the risks associated with GenAI use, including the potential for generating fabricated legal authority, the attorney admitted to “not fully vetting or verifying the governing law or the citations within the submitted memorandum.” The attorney took responsibility, acknowledged the seriousness of his actions, and informed the court that he had undertaken remedial measures, including enrolling in CLEs on GenAI-assisted legal practice and developing strengthened verification protocols. Citing the Fifth Circuit’s observation that courts are increasingly “burdened [with] addressing AI hallucinations in court filings,” Judge Edwards emphasized that a lawyer's duty remains “unchanged” regardless of whether GenAI is used, as the lawyer must “ensure that the legal propositions and authority generated are trustworthy.” While commending the attorney for his “candor, honesty, and the remedial measures he has undertaken,” the court found that these mitigating factors did not excuse the attorney’s conduct, as Rule 11 requires attorneys to verify the accuracy of submissions to the court at a minimum. The court imposed a $1,000 monetary sanction to be paid personally by the attorney.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Clark v. Corecivic, Inc., No. CIV-25-775-SLP, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 50645 (W.D. Okla. Mar. 6, 2026), the pro se plaintiff mischaracterized case law in his response to the motion to dismiss, in what the court found was “likely from the use of AI software without independent verification of case citations.” The court issued an order admonishing the plaintiff and warned that further misconduct could result in sanctions. Though the plaintiff apologized and promised “that he had reviewed all authorities ‘to ensure their accuracy,’” he made similar errors in his surreply. Magistrate Judge Stephens again warned the plaintiff of his responsibility to submit accurate citations to the court and recommended that the court grant summary judgment to the defendant on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In In re Domestic P'ship of Torres Campos & Munoz, 2026 Cal. App. LEXIS 130 (Cal. Ct. App. Mar. 5, 2026), the court considered an appeal from a family court order and addressing respondent’s counsel’s citation to fictitious legal authorities in a letter sent to opposing counsel, in the respondent’s declaration, and in briefs filed with the appellate court. Respondent’s counsel admitted to citing non-existent cases, stating that one “may have” been generated by GenAI and that the other was sourced from a “Reddit article that was authored by a non-attorney blogger,” and that she “did not verify” those authorities. The family court then issued an order which relied upon those non-existent cases first cited by respondent (and then later cited by petitioner); the appellate court ruled it constituted an abuse of discretion for the family court to rely “in material part on fictional case authorities in rendering a decision or making an order.” However, the fact that the petitioner had also cited the fictitious cases and failed to identify them earlier (before belatedly raising them as grounds for reversal) constituted forfeiture of that claim of error. In addressing the GenAI misuse, the appellate court noted that—despite respondent counsel’s admission to citing the cases in question—she “doubled down” in her defense of the fake citations even when confronted with them by opposing counsel. The court found this conduct to fall “far short of [the attorney’s] duty of candor with the court” under California’s Rules of Professional Conduct and the Rules of the Court. As a result, the appellate court imposed $5,000 in sanctions on respondent’s counsel (as well as ordering her to report the sanction to the state bar), particularly because she both “persisted in and aggravated the misconduct” by defending the fictitious citations, and because she “still has not been entirely forthcoming” about how one of the citations was originally sourced.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms
judge: Judge Martin N. Buchanan | Acting Presiding Judge Joan K. Irion | Judge Truc T. Do

rg_summary: In Woodward Harbor L.L.C. v. City of Mandeville, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 45132 (E.D. La. Mar. 5, 2026), following an order to show cause, the court sanctioned one of the plaintiffs’ attorneys for using GenAI to draft an opposition brief that contained “citations to at least eleven cases that either did not exist or were incorrectly summarized, relied on, or quoted from.” Even after the defendant brought the nonexistent case law to the court’s and plaintiff’s attention through its reply brief, the plaintiffs took no corrective action. The court had previously dismissed the plaintiffs’ complaint with prejudice for failure to state a claim, noting the numerous hallucinated citations and concluding they were likely the product of GenAI, and ordered the attorney to show cause as to why he should not  be sanctioned for the hallucinate citations. Following the order to show cause hearing (in which the filing attorney admitted to using GenAI tools and being “unaware” of the risk of hallucinations), the court remarked that it was “troubled” that an experienced attorney could be so unfamiliar with “an issue so well-publicized in the legal community.” Although the court recognized the attorney’s candor and agreement to repay the relevant client’s fees, the court nonetheless imposed a $1,000 personal sanction on the offending attorney and ordered him to attend two hours of CLE on GenAI. The court also declined to sanction three other attorneys whose names appeared on the brief (based on their declarations that the filing attorney bore sole responsibility for verifying the brief’s accuracy), but reminded them of their “personal, nondelegable responsibility” under FRCP 11.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Carroll v. Univ. of Ky., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 73704 (E.D. Ky. Mar. 4, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed a response in opposition to the defendants’ motion for partial dismissal that contained a citation to a nonexistent case. After the defendants identified the error in their reply, the plaintiff filed a notice of errata correcting the citation errors and substituting different case law in support of his position. The court denied the plaintiff’s motion for leave to file a sur-reply, noting that he had already addressed the defendants’ allegation in his notice of errata. While the court appreciated the plaintiff’s correction, Magistrate Judge Atkins warned that continued citation of nonexistent case law “may result in sanctions such as the pleading being stricken, filing restrictions being imposed, or recommendation that his case be dismissed.”  The court recommended granting the defendants’ motion to dismiss in part and denying it in part on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Vernieri v. Dyno Nobile Ams., Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 73221 (D. Utah Mar. 4, 2026), the pro se plaintiff’s opposition to the defendants’ motion to dismiss contained citations to non-existent cases that “appear[]” to be GenAI hallucinations. The court warned the plaintiff that, within the 10th Circuit, courts have dismissed plaintiffs’ complaints in full without any ruling on the merits when parties “misuse . . . generative AI tools.” Though Judge Pead declined to do so here, he cautioned that future GenAI misuse could lead to “dismissal of the action and any other appropriate sanctions.” The court recommended that the defendants’ motion to dismiss be granted on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Dixon v. Multicare Health Sys., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 44646 (W.D. Wash. Mar. 4, 2026), the pro se plaintiff repeatedly cited nonexistent cases and cases that did not support the propositions for which they were offered in filings submitted to the court. The defendant requested that the court sanction the plaintiff for his improper filings, Judge Settle noted that while a pro se litigant is not held to the same standard as a licensed attorney, pro se litigants “must follow the same rules of procedure that govern other litigants” and “should not be treated more favorably than parties with attorneys of record.” The court observed that it has “no formal rule against the use of [GenAI] to write pleadings and briefs,” but emphasized that parties are bound by Fed. R. Civ. P. 11, which requires parties to certify that their “legal contentions are warranted by existing law or by a nonfrivolous argument for extending, modifying, or reversing existing law or for establishing new law.” Finding that the plaintiff’s “repeated and presumably intentional failure to verify cited authority supports an award of sanctions,” the court admonished the plaintiff for his inappropriate conduct and warned that monetary sanctions may be awarded if, in the future, the plaintiff repeats this behavior. On the merits, the court granted in part and denied in part the defendant’s motion to dismiss.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In in re Est. of Carey, 2026 Wash. App. LEXIS 418 (Wash. Ct. App. Mar. 3, 2026), the pro se appellant’s briefs contained citations to “incorrect, chimerical, or inapplicable case law” throughout, including citations to cases that did not exist and misquotations or misrepresentations of the holdings of cases that did exist. The court declined to review arguments premised on fictitious authority, holding that citations to fictitious legal authority “equates to a lack of legal authority” under Rule of Appellate Procedure 10.3(a)(6), which requires parties to provide citations to relevant legal authority in support of their arguments. The court also found the appeal “so devoid of merit that there was no possibility for reversal,” thus rendering it frivolous. While the court declined to impose independent sanctions for the appellant's use of fictitious case law and failure to comply with the Rules of Appellate Procedure, it did impose sanctions for the appellant’s frivolous appeal.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties
judge: Judge John O. Cooney | Judge Tracy Arlene Staab | Judge Megan K. Murphy

rg_summary: Magistrate Judge Donahue’s standing order in D.M. v. Vergil Services, Inc. requires parties who use GenAI tools (e.g., ChatGPT, Harvey, CoCounsel, or Google Bard) “to generate any portion of a brief, pleading, or other filing” to attach a declaration disclosing GenAI use and certifying that the content is accurate and “complies with the filer’s Rule 11 obligations.” See No. 2:25-cv-05102-PD, Dkt. No. 37 at 5 (C.D. Cal. Mar. 2, 2026).
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Perry v. Exeter Fin. LLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 39515 (D. Ariz. Feb. 26, 2026), the pro se plaintiff’s opposition contained citations to two cases where “the citation provided does not return the case identified” and two fake quotations attributed to real cases. The court warned that regardless of whether the errors were the result of GenAI, future citations to “fictitious cases” will result in sanctions under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In United States v. Ponce, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 39550 (D. Nev. Feb. 26, 2026), the court observed that defendant’s “motions and replies are filled with hallucinogenic citations to non-existent cases.” The court warned the defendant that if the defendant relied upon GenAI, it is “his responsibility” to check the accuracy of his case citations, or he may be subject to sanctions under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. The court denied the defendant’s motion to exempt workers’ compensation settlement without prejudice on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Hicks v. City of Albany, 2026 Ga. App. LEXIS 115 (Ga. Ct. App. Feb. 26, 2026), the appellant’s counsel included citations to two cases that the court “could not locate” in the initial brief on appeal. Counsel failed to provide an explanation until the court issued an order. The court declined to issue sanctions because the appellant’s counsel took responsibility and apologized for including citations which she “believe[d] came from an AI platform.” Judge Rickman cautioned that inaccurate filings may result in sanctions. The court affirmed the judgment on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Hunt v. True Sky Fed. Credit Union, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 38632 (D. Kan. Feb. 25, 2026), the pro se plaintiff cited to a non-existent case in his brief. The court made the plaintiff aware of the court’s standing order on the use of AI in court filings, entered after the plaintiff filed his brief. The standing order requires a “a litigant to review and verify the accuracy of all content filed with the court that was drafted or assisted using an AI tool.” Judge Broomes warned that future filings which violate the standing order could result in sanctions. The court granted in part and took under advisement in part the defendant’s motion to dismiss on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Kettering Adventist Healthcare v. Collier, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 38680 (S.D. Ohio Feb. 25, 2026), the defendants cited “at least twelve cases that either do not exist or do not remotely stand for the purported propositions of law.” See also Kettering Adventist Healthcare v. Collier, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 124 (S.D. Ohio Jan. 2, 2026) (detailing violations). The attorneys continued citing fabricated citations even after opposing counsel brought the nonexistent cases to their attention. In response to the order to show cause, the attorneys made “timely and contrite filings to the Court, including intended affirmative steps to prevent future errors.” But despite these promises, the attorneys proceeded to submit “patent misstatements and mischaracterizations of the law” to Magistrate Judge Silvain in their mediation statements in the underlying case, “[t]he breadth and depth of [the attorneys’] continued malfeasance leav[ing] [the Court] almost at a loss for words.” Judge Rice concluded that the attorney’s actions “constitute the most egregious, inexplicable, and repeated violations of Rule 11(b) by any attorneys that have ever appeared before the undersigned,” and that the attorneys also violated Ohio R. Prof’l Conduct 3.3(a)(1). The court imposed sanctions against the attorneys and found them in contempt of court for their misrepresentations to Magistrate Judge Silvain. As sanction, the court ordered the defendant’s motion to dismiss stricken without prejudice, and imposed a monetary sanction of $5,000 against one attorney and $2,500 against the other (who appeared later in the case). Judge Rice also referred the attorneys’ conduct to the Supreme Court of Ohio’s Office of Disciplinary Counsel and requested that the Chief Judge determine whether additional sanction is necessary for the contempt of court.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Mau v. Honolulu, No. 24cv0253-GPC-WRP (D. Haw. Feb. 24, 2026), the defendant’s counsel cited “fake cases” and cases that “do not support the propositions stated,” prompting the court to issue an order to show cause. In response to the order to show cause, the attorneys admitted the mistakes and accepted responsibility, but asserted that they did not use GenAI. The court found that a hearing was not necessary since there was no dispute that the attorneys violated Rule 11 by submitting fictitious citations to the court. Because the attorneys accepted responsibility and did not defend the inaccuracies, Judge Curiel imposed a “modest” sanction of $1,000 each against the two attorneys.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Lee v. Cap. One Bank, No. 2:25-cv-540, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 52839 (D. Utah Feb. 24, 2026), the defendant raised concerns about the pro se plaintiff’s reliance on GenAI for his filings and requested that the court “disregard any AI-generated portions of Plaintiff's opposition to the extent they are inaccurate or hallucinated” or that the plaintiff “relies primarily on such content without providing specific factual support for its claims.” The court declined, instead admonishing the plaintiff “to take greater care to avoid misrepresentations in his filings or face possible sanctions.” Magistrate Judge Pead recommended that the court grant the defendant’s motion to dismiss on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Wells Fargo Bank, N.A. v. Dean, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 43000 (E.D. Tex. Feb. 24, 2026), two of the pro se defendant’s motions submitted to the court contained citations to nonexistent cases and cases that did not stand for the cited propositions. The court suspected that the errors stemmed from the use of GenAI, noting that the problem has become “a regrettably common problem, wasting party and court resources alike.” Magistrate Judge Davis emphasized that by signing and filing documents with the court, parties certify under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11(b)(2) that their legal contentions are warranted by existing law, and that false citations “contradict that certification and are tantamount to dishonesty.” The court further noted that the defendant's pro se status “in no way relieves her of responsibility for the accuracy and quality of the documents she submits to the court” under Local Rule CV-11(g). The court warned that citing nonexistent cases “undermines the integrity of the judiciary and the court's trust in litigants” and is “unacceptable,” and that the defendant will be subject to sanctions if she files another document containing hallucinated or otherwise nonexistent citations. On unrelated grounds, the court dismissed one of the Defendant’s motions and recommended the denial of another.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Yaroshevsky v. Karlin, No. 25-CV-2747, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 37943 (E.D.N.Y. Feb. 23, 2026), the pro se plaintiff’s complaint and motions contained nonexistent or misleading citations, leading Judge Gujarati (who referred the case to Magistrate Judge Eichenholtz) to warn that further misconduct could lead to sanctions. The plaintiff then continued to file documents containing nonexistent or misleading citations and Judge Gujarati issued another warning. The plaintiff did not attempt to correct the inaccurate filings, even after the defendants indicated they would be filing a motion for sanctions. The plaintiff eventually and belatedly admitted to the inaccuracy of some of the citations and the use of the Gemini GenAI tool (after “at least five prior declarations to the contrary during the conference and numerous representations over the prior seven months to other judges and counsel”). The plaintiff then sought leave to amend the complaint and an opposition to the defendant’s motion for sanctions, but that motion itself also contained fabricated authorities. Judge Eichenholtz recommended granting the defendant’s motion for sanctions including dismissal with prejudice and fees related to the motion, or granting the defendant’s motion to dismiss and dismissing the complaint with prejudice on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Hawkins v. I.C. Sys., Inc., Civil Action No. 4:25-CV-2194, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 36089 (S.D. Tex. Feb. 23, 2026), the court concluded that the pro se plaintiff cited nonexistent authorities after the court “unsuccessfully exhausted efforts to locate these cases.” Magistrate Judge Palermo reminded the plaintiff of his obligations under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 and cautioned that violations of the rule could result in sanctions. The court recommended granting in part the defendant’s motion to dismiss on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Reynolds v. Progressive Techs., Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 69377 (W.D. Tenn. Feb. 20, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed a response to the defendant’s motion to dismiss that contained multiple manufactured quotations attributed to real cases and citations to completely fictitious cases. While the defendant separately requested that the plaintiff be ordered to show cause concerning his alleged use of GenAI-hallucinated case citations, Magistrate Judge Christoff decided not to impose or recommend sanctions at that time, and instead “admonished [the plaintiff] in the strongest possible terms that fictional case law citations are entirely inappropriate.” She warned that “any future infractions may result in the imposition of sanctions, up to and including the dismissal of his case,” and ordered the plaintiff to review any legal citations for accuracy before including them in future filings. The court recommended granting in part and denying in part the motion to dismiss on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In in re: Marrett v. Aroostook Cnty. Fed. Sav. & Loan (Bankr. D. Mass. Feb. 19, 2026), the pro se debtors cited a nonexistent case in their complaint as the only authority supporting one of their causes of action. The court therefore concluded that “the Debtors have not provided . . . any authority to support a cause of action.” The court granted the defendant’s motion to dismiss for failure to state a claim.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Veramancini v. Cnty. of Orange, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 35478 (C.D. Cal. Feb. 19, 2026), the court sanctioned plaintiffs' counsel for submitting briefing containing inaccurate case quotations and characterizations indicative of GenAI use. The court first noted potential GenAI misuse in response to inaccuracies in counsel's motion to dismiss briefing stage, after which counsel submitted a declaration acknowledging the errors and pledging corrective measures. However, in a subsequent briefing, opposing counsel informed the court that plaintiff’s counsel had misrepresented the holding of a state supreme court case, and the court again raised the issue at a hearing. Despite these warnings, counsel's summary judgment briefing repeated the same types of errors, attributing quotes to cases that did not contain those quotes and misrepresenting the holding of an appellate case. Throughout the proceedings, counsel repeatedly told the court that he did not use GenAI to draft his briefs, but ultimately acknowledged using Google Gemini for “grammar, spelling, and conciseness.” The court found that sanctions were appropriate under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11, emphasizing that “citing nonexistent case law or misrepresenting the holdings of a case is making a false statement to a court, regardless of whether [GenAI] suggested the content.” While counsel represented that his errors were “unintentional,” the court noted that it had already allowed him “substantial latitude” by not issuing sanctions in response to the first or second round of misrepresentations. The court sanctioned counsel by ordering him to (1) pay $2,000 personally to the Clerk of the Court and (2) serve a copy of the order on the State Bar of California.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Varsobia Home Care Servs., LLC v. Com. Borrowing Fund, LLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 34499 (C.D. Cal. Feb. 19, 2026), the court suspected that the plaintiff prepared its complaint with the assistance of GenAI, along with several other threshold deficiencies. The court noted that complaint contained unfilled GenAI placeholder text (including bracketed instructions such as “[describe specific irregularities, e.g., failure to provide required documentation, improper notice, or other procedural deficiencies].”). Judge Scarsi ordered that any party in this matter who uses GenAI in connection with filings must attach a separate declaration disclosing the use and certifying that the filer has reviewed and verified the content as “accurate and in compliance with Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11.” The declaration must identify which portions of the filing incorporate GenAI outputs. The court also warned that presenting inaccurate or undeclared GenAI outputs may result in sanctions. On unrelated grounds, the court ordered the plaintiff to show cause as to why the complaint should not be dismissed without prejudice.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Pena v. Wells Fargo Bank, N.A., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 33823 (S.D. Fla. Feb. 19, 2026), the pro se plaintiffs’ renewed motion for preliminary injunction cited a real case for a proposition it does not support and referenced a non-existent page of that opinion, leading the court to suspect that the plaintiffs obtained their analysis from “an unreliable source, such as [GenAI].” The court noted that it had “previously admonished Plaintiffs for potentially using [GenAI] hallucinated legal citations,” and warned that if the plaintiffs include any other false or inaccurate legal citations that the court suspects were GenAI-generated, it will order them to appear in person to explain those citations and why they should not be sanctioned. The court recommended denying the motion on unrelated grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Fletcher v. Experian Info. Sols., Inc., No. 25-20086, 2026 U.S. App. LEXIS 4834 (5th Cir. Feb. 18, 2026), the court found that the plaintiff’s counsel used GenAI to draft her reply brief and failed to verify its accuracy. The brief contained at least 16 fabricated quotations and 5 serious misrepresentations of law or fact. In response to the order to show cause, the plaintiff’s counsel denied GenAI use, and asserted that she “relied on publicly available versions of the cases, which [she] believed were accurate.” The court did not find this explanation credible and asked for additional explanation. The plaintiff’s counsel then admitted to using GenAI, but only to “help organize and structure [her] arguments and to break up overly long paragraphs.” She attributed the inaccurate quotations to “‘paraphrased summaries,’ which she claims to have ‘mistakenly believed . . . reflected the actual language of the cases,’” but the court could not find such summaries. The court also concluded that because counsel “did not explain the steps she took to verify” assertions in her brief, the court “concludes that she took none.” The court therefore found that she use GenAI to draft her brief and failed to check it for accuracy, and thus imposed a $2,500 sanction under Fed. R. App. P. 46(c) and the court’s “inherent power to impose sanctions for abuse of the judicial process.” The court noted that “[h]ad [counsel] accepted responsibility and been more forthcoming, it is likely that the court would have imposed lesser sanctions.”
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms
judge: Chief Judge Jennifer Walker Elrod | Judge Jerry E. Smith | Judge Cory T. Wilson

rg_summary: In EFD USA, Inc. v. Band Pro Film & Digital Inc., 2026 Cal. App. Unpub. LEXIS 1114 (Cal. Ct. App. Feb. 18, 2026), the appellate court identified several “fabricated quotations and inaccurate descriptions of legal authority” in the appellant’s briefing. The appellant’s lead attorney had used a GenAI “consultant” to prepare a draft opening brief and later used an “unidentified ‘AI system’” to shorten the brief to meet the word limit, but failed to confirm whether the system changed any citations during that process. The opening brief contained several fabricated quotations from real cases, including a leading case cited for a proposition it did not address and a purported quotation that did not appear in the cited authority. The respondents’ brief identified the deficiencies, but the appellant failed to acknowledge or explain the inaccuracies in its reply. In fact, the appellant’s reply brief also contained at least two fabricated quotations. The court then issued an order to show cause why the appellant’s attorneys should not be sanctioned for pursuing a frivolous appeal. In response, the lead attorney accepted responsibility for the errors and attributed them to the GenAI system he used to shorten the briefs. The court emphasized that it is “a fundamental duty of attorneys to read the legal authorities they cite in appellate briefs” and that counsel “plainly” did not read the cases before filing. Finding that the fabricated citations violated the California Rules of Court, which require that arguments be supported “with citations to real (as opposed to fabricated) legal authority,” the court imposed a $900 sanction against the lead attorney alone, payable to the clerk of the court. The court noted that while the deficiencies were “similar in kind” to those in a recent case where it imposed a $10,000 sanction, they were “far less widespread.” The court affirmed lower court’s judgment on all grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms
judge: Judge Mark Hanasono | Judge Lee S. Edmon | Judge Rashida A. Adams

rg_summary: In Martin v. ODS Cmty. Dental Ins., No. 3:26-cv-00298-AB, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 32712 (D. Or. Feb. 18, 2026), the court noticed that the pro se plaintiff’s motion for a temporary restraining order contained inaccurate or incomplete citations. Judge Baggio warned that “to the extent that Plaintiff is using [GenAI] to aid him in his briefing … the [c]ourt will not tolerate further inaccurate or incomplete citations to legal authority.” The court denied the plaintiff’s motion for a TRO on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Jones v. Target Corp., No. 3:23-cv-01301-JR, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 47888 (D. Or. Feb. 18, 2026), on review of the defendant’s motion for terminating sanctions against the pro se plaintiff, the court determined that the plaintiff “filed baseless motions, withheld basic discovery, and relied on ‘hallucinated’ case citations.” In assessing whether terminating sanctions were appropriate, the court considered, among other things, the plaintiff’s behavior that caused unnecessary delays in the case, the plaintiff’s failure to participate in discovery, and the availability of lesser sanctions. The court determined that lesser sanctions “would seemingly have no effect” and recommended terminating sanctions.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Walder v. Experian Info. Sols., Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 33215 (E.D. Tex. Feb. 18, 2026), Judge J. Campbell Barker reviewed de novo and adopted Magistrate Judge Mitchell’s report and recommendation (2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 34005) denying the pro se plaintiff's motion for summary judgment. The plaintiff objected on three grounds, including that her citation of fictitious cases was “unintentional, not made in bad faith, and not intended to mislead the Court.” The court noted its appreciation of the plaintiff’s clarification, while reiterating the magistrate judge’s warning “that further citation to nonexistent cases and noncompliance with Local Rule CV-11(g) may result in sanctions, including her pleadings and other papers being struck, filing restrictions, dismissal of her claims, and monetary penalties.” The court overruled the plaintiff's objections, denied the motion without prejudice, and permitted the plaintiff to refile by the court's dispositive motion deadline.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: The Oklahoma Court of Criminal Appeals adopted Rule 1.17 governing the use of GenAI in documents filed before the court, entitled “Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence.” See In re Addition of A New Rule to the Rules of the Ct. of Crim. Appeals, 2026 OK CR 7 (Okla. Crim. App. Feb. 18, 2026).  Rule 1.17 defines GenAI as “any type of artificial intelligence that generates content or data in response to a prompt or query by a user.” The rule requires that when GenAI has been “used in the drafting of any document for filing in this Court,” the party or their counsel must “ensure that any portion of the document produced or modified by [GenAI], whether in whole or in part, has been verified as accurate by a person responsible for the document.” Courts may issue sanctions (including, but not limited to, waiver of issues, striking documents, and contempt) for failure to comply. In adopting the rule, the court recognized GenAI's potential to “produce misinformation or even to hallucinate.” Presiding Judge Lumpkin specially concurred, emphasizing that a lawyer’s signature on any document constitutes a certification that “all citations of authority, and cases in the document are true and correct, and all quoted language is accurate and correctly referenced,” and that any document that proves to be incorrect in its use of legal authority “subjects the lawyer to sanctions and disciplinary actions.”
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
judge: Presiding Judge Gary L. Lumpkin | Vice Presiding Judge William J. Musseman | Judge Robert L. Hudson | Judge Scott Rowland

rg_summary: In Mardis v. Dealer Loyalty Prot., No. 2:25-cv-1237, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 32806 (S.D. Ohio Feb. 18, 2026), the defendants alerted the court that the pro se plaintiff likely cited cases hallucinated by GenAI in his motions. Judge Sargus denied the defendants’ motion for sanctions but warned that citing to AI-generated caselaw violates Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. The court directed the plaintiff to cease the behavior and warned that further violations could result in sanctions. The court denied the plaintiff’s motions on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Robertson v. TrueAccord Corp., No. 4:25-CV-2816 (S.D. Tex. Feb. 18, 2026), the pro se plaintiff's response to the defendant's motion to dismiss contained several inaccurate or false case citations. The court noted that the citations “mush together case names, action numbers, and database identifiers from real decisions,” raising the concern that the plaintiff was using GenAI to draft her filings. While recognizing that the plaintiff may not be aware of the risks that GenAI tools pose, and acknowledging the difficulty pro se litigants may face without access to legal databases to verify such citations, the court emphasized that pro se status “does not exempt a party from compliance with relevant rules of procedural and substantive law.” The court reminded the plaintiff that filings containing false citations or quotations violate Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 regardless of whether they are GenAI-generated, and that she must also comply with the court's General Order 2025-04 regarding the use of GenAI in court filings. The court warned that failure to comply may result in sanctions, including monetary sanctions and dismissal of the plaintiff's claims with prejudice.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In in re Escobedo, Nos. 25-12572 MER, 25-1223 MER, 2026 Bankr. LEXIS 390 (Bankr. D. Colo. Feb. 17, 2026), the court noticed that the pro se defendant cited a fake case and included “what appears to be prompts from some artificial intelligence guidance” in his motion to dismiss. Judge Romero warned that GenAI use without verifying the accuracy of citations may result in sanctions. The court granted in part and denied in part the defendant’s motion to dismiss on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Rios v. Puente Hills Ford, LLC, No. B344199, 2026 Cal. App. Unpub. LEXIS 1060 (Cal. Ct. App. Feb. 17, 2026), the pro se plaintiff misquoted ten cases and cited two nonexistent cases in his opening brief. The court warned that such conduct violates Cal. R. Ct. 8.204, which requires that all litigants (including pro se parties) verify accuracy of citations in briefs. Judge Adams declined to impose sanctions but cautioned that continued fabricated quotations and citations may result in sanctions. The court affirmed the judgment on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In his Standing Order Regarding Civil Litigation (N.D. Ga. Feb. 17, 2026), Judge Grimberg addresses GenAI use by stating that while parties may any AI tools, “only human beings will be held responsible for the outcome.” If a filing contains factual or legal errors, the party and lawyers who filed it will be held accountable, “includ[ing] each and every lawyer listed on the signature block of the filing.” The standing order does not impose any disclosure or certification requirements related to GenAI use.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Picon-Diaz v. Bondi, No. 25-9530, 2026 U.S. App. LEXIS 4417 (10th Cir. Feb. 13, 2026), the petitioners’ counsel relied on or quoted from a nonexistent citation “multiple times” in their brief. The court could not find “any relevant federal case similarly captioned or containing any of the quotations that counsel attributes to this apparently nonexistent case” and suspected GenAI use without proper review. Judge Kelly “disregard[ed] [the p]etitioners’ reliance on this case.” The court declined to impose sanctions but warned the petitioners’ counsel and all attorneys “of the responsibility to ensure that citations to legal authority are not fabrications.” The court denied the petition for review.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
judge: Judge Joel McElroy Carson | Judge Bobby Ray Badlock | Judge Paul J. Kelly Jr.

rg_summary: Chief Judge Chase's Administrative Order No. 26-10, titled “Disclosure of Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence by Attorneys and Self-Represented Litigants,” requires attorneys and self-represented litigants appearing before the Circuit and County Courts of the Eighteenth Judicial Circuit to disclose the use of any GenAI tool in the preparation of pleadings, motions, memoranda, responses, proposed orders, or other court records. The order notes that such tools may generate inaccurate assertions or citations, and that the court “should not have to parse case citations and parentheticals to discern whether cases exist.” Filings prepared with GenAI assistance must therefore include a certification stating that all factual assertions, legal authority, and citations have been independently reviewed and verified for accuracy, and that the filer accepts full responsibility for the filing's contents. The order emphasizes that GenAI use does not relieve any party of the duty of candor to the tribunal, the obligation to independently verify all factual assertions and legal authority, or compliance with applicable statutes, procedural rules, and the Rules Regulating the Florida Bar. All GenAI-generated content is deemed the work product of the filing party. The order also prohibits the submission of fictitious, fabricated, or hallucinated content; reliance on GenAI-generated citations without independent verification; misrepresentation of GenAI-generated content as independently researched authority; and failure to disclose GenAI use as required by the order, noting that failure to comply may result in sanctions.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Mahwikizi v. Uber Techs., Inc. & Rasier, LLC, 2026 U.S. App. LEXIS 4411 (7th Cir. Feb. 13, 2026), the court noted that it could not find two of the cases the pro se plaintiff cited in his reply brief. The court did not impose sanctions but reminded litigants that whether or not the errors were the result of GenAI use, they must “carefully review their submissions before filing in this court.” The court affirmed the district court’s dismissal for failure to prosecute on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
judge: Judge Frank H. Easterbrook | Judge David Frank Hamilton | Judge Doris Lenea Pryor

rg_summary: In Allah-El v. Columbia Univ., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 30842 (S.D.N.Y. Feb. 13, 2026), the court denied with prejudice the plaintiff’s counsel’s motion for admission pro hac vice. In a prior related action before Judge Denise L. Cote, counsel had filed default judgment requests that were “incomprehensible” and which contained “numerous errors” and “an entire conversation between plaintiff's counsel and ChatGPT.” After noting that counsel’s submissions were clearly drafted by GenAI, Judge Cote ordered counsel to appear and explain why she should not be sanctioned for this conduct, among other deficiencies, and ultimately dismissed that action without prejudice. When the plaintiff refiled the case that was assigned to Judge Rearden, counsel violated Judge Cote’s order to immediately bring the transcript of the prior proceedings to the new presiding judge’s attention. Counsel also made several other procedural errors related to her pro hac vice status and other filings. The court found that counsel's repeated errors and noncompliance with court orders demonstrated that she was unfit to be admitted pro hac vice and denied her application with prejudice. In conjunction, the court directed the plaintiff to retain qualified counsel or file a letter indicating that he intends to proceed pro se.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Rechlicz v. Price Point Builders, LLC, 2026 Ind. App. LEXIS 48 (Ind. App. Feb. 12, 2026), the appellants requested that the court sanction the appellee for its improper citations in its brief. The court found that while the appellee’s brief contained case citations with typos and one case that does not exist, the mistakes seemed to be “inadvertent typographical errors” that “did not hinder [the court’s] review of [appellee’s] arguments.” Judge DeBoer admonished the appellee for failing to properly proofread the brief but declined to impose sanctions and affirmed the trial court’s judgment on unrelated grounds.
rg_tags: Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
judge: Judge Mary DeBoer | Judge Elane B. Brown | Judge Robert R. Altice Jr.

rg_summary: In City of Dickinson v. Helgeson, 2026 ND 34 (N.D. Feb. 12, 2026), the North Dakota Supreme Court granted the City’s request for sanctions against the pro se appellant for his citations to several fictitious cases in his brief in support of his appeal from the district court’s order designating him a vexatious litigant. The state Supreme Court determined that the appellant had similarly submitted fictitious citations to the district court. Judge Crothers noted that while North Dakota courts embrace the use of technology as a means of improving access to justice, litigants must verify arguments and authorities generated by these tools. The court concluded that the appellant was “responsible for the consequences of his errors” regardless of whether they were the result of GenAI. Judge Crothers awarded the City $500 as sanction for the appellant’s violation of N.D.R.App.P. 28(l), which requires that briefs presented to the court be accurate. The court further warned all litigants that citing to nonexistent cases could result in sanctions “appropriate under the circumstances.” The court affirmed the district court’s order on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Virgil v. Experian Info. Sols. Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 29090 (S.D. Ind. Feb. 12, 2026), the plaintiff’s attorneys cited to nonexistent caselaw and caselaw that did not relate to the propositions for which it was cited in their response to the defendant’s motion to dismiss. The plaintiff’s attorneys continued to cite to fabricated caselaw even after the defendants raised the issue. At the Show Cause Hearing, the court determined that only one of the plaintiff’s attorneys was responsible for the filings, as the other had never seen the briefs and did not consent to the addition of his signature. The filing attorney took full responsibility and informed the court that he had since implemented preventative measures going forward. Magistrate Judge Dinsmore found that the attorney’s failure to “make the requisite reasonable inquiry into the law” and to “take the most basic of actions—checking the citations in his own briefs,” regardless of whether GenAI was the source of the errors, violated Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. In imposing sanctions, the court considered the amount of sanctions awarded in similar cases and concluded that the “imposition of modest sanctions has failed to act as a deterrent.” Magistrate Judge Dinsmore recommended $10,000 in personal sanctions and referred the matter to the Chief Judge for consideration of further discipline. The court also ordered the attorney to provide a copy of the order to his client.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Chumpitaz-Morales v. Bondi, 2026 U.S. App. LEXIS 4197 (10th Cir. Feb. 11, 2026), the court suspected that the pro se petitioner used GenAI in drafting her brief because it misrepresented several cited cases. Judge Baldock noted that the court had the power to impose sanctions in this instance but chose not to, though warned that “parties who use AI products should know they can and do ‘hallucinate’ legal citations.” The court denied the petition on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
judge: Judge Joel McElroy Carson | Judge Bobby Ray Badlock | Judge Paul J. Kelly Jr.
